summary: Gruber previews Macworld Expo 2010, the first without Apple's participation, assessing whether the show can survive. He argues that Apple's keynote was never really about the trade show itself, and that the real value of Macworld lies in its small exhibitors and conference sessions. He compares Apple's show floor presence to an anchor department store at a mall, acknowledging the risk of its absence. Ultimately, he expresses cautious optimism that demand exists for a Mac-focused community event independent of Apple's direct participation.
key_points: Apple's absence effectively makes Macworld a new event, and IDG was smart to change the date to emphasize the fresh start
Apple's keynote drew media attention to Apple itself, not to the trade show — the two were always somewhat disconnected
Apple's show floor presence functioned like an anchor store at a mall, drawing foot traffic that benefited smaller exhibitors
The real value of walking the show floor has always been discovering products from small companies where the booth staff are the actual creators
Macworld has quietly become a successful conference in its own right, with tracks spanning programming to graphic design
money_quotes: Apple's traditional presence at Macworld was so large, both figuratively (with the attention paid to their keynote address) and literally (with their massive booth on the show floor), that their absence has effectively rendered Macworld a new event.
It's not like Apple hasn't given us much to talk about recently — hello, iPad — it just wasn't announced at Macworld itself.
The role they played on the show floor is very much analogous, I think, to the role played by a big department store like Macy's or Nordstrom at a shopping mall.
The ones where the employees manning the booth are the engineers and designers who made the product they're promoting.
I've been to a bunch of Macworld Expos and I never once failed to discover at least one fascinating product by walking the show floor.
Nothing could replace a Steve Jobs keynote address, so, wisely, they're not trying.
But is there demand for a days-long nerdfest for Mac and iPhone professionals and aficionados? I say yes.
key_insight: Apple's departure from Macworld Expo didn't kill the show's reason to exist — it just stripped away the media spectacle that had been obscuring the genuine community value underneath.
